name: Italian sweet red pepper (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fresh weight (ripe red Capsicum annuum, sweet/bell type)

components.name: D-fructose
components.evidence: Dominant free sugar in ripe red pepper; fructose > glucose at full ripeness (fructose ~51-58% of total sugars)

components.name: D-glucose
components.evidence: Second free sugar; glucose ~42-49% of total sugars at full ripeness (Aguero/Plants 2023, doi:10.3390/plants12020231)

components.name: sucrose
components.evidence: Sucrose is hydrolysed during ripening and is near-undetectable in fully ripe red fruit (present at trace); total sugars ~4.2 g/100g

components.name: citric acid
components.evidence: Dominant organic acid in pepper (24.6-49.5% of total); 1.82-5.60 mg/g FW range, rises with ripening so red-ripe value taken near mid-upper (~3.5 mg/g)

components.name: L-malic acid
components.evidence: 0.99-1.88 mg/g FW (12.7-21.6% of total organic acids); declines with ripening so red-ripe value at lower end (~1.2 mg/g)

components.name: fumaric acid
components.evidence: Reported at 1.48-3.71 mg/g FW (18.8-33.8% of total organic acids); high vs typical fruit, value is a conservative estimate within the reported range; TCA intermediate

components.name: 2-oxoglutarate (alpha-ketoglutarate)
components.evidence: 0.70-1.43 mg/g FW (10.4-16.8% of total organic acids); TCA-cycle carbon/N source

components.name: oxalic acid
components.evidence: <0.7 mg/g FW (<8% of total organic acids); minor

components.name: quinic acid
components.source: Capsicum pericarp phytochemistry (81.3 mg/100g DW, ~8% dry matter)
components.evidence: Minor organic acid, converted from dry-weight pericarp value

components.name: L-ascorbate (vitamin C)
components.source: USDA FDC 170108; Italian Campania sweet pepper PMC7346132 (0.82-1.20 mg/g in red varieties)
components.evidence: Red sweet pepper is among the richest vegetable sources of vitamin C; also a defining organic acid

components.name: total protein
components.evidence: Basis for protein-derived amino-acid profile below

components.name: L-aspartate
components.evidence: Most abundant amino acid in pepper (free + protein)

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: Second most abundant; major N/C source

components.name: L-serine
components.evidence: Corrects current 'D-Serine' entry; food L-serine

components.name: L-methionine
components.evidence: Limiting sulfur amino acid

components.name: glycine betaine
components.evidence: Present as compatible-solute osmolyte; not quantified in USDA

components.name: linoleic acid (18:2 n-6)
components.evidence: Main PUFA; total fat only 0.30 g/100g

components.name: palmitic acid (16:0)
components.evidence: Main saturated FA (total saturated ~0.06 g)

components.name: oleic acid (18:1 n-9)
components.evidence: Minor MUFA

components.name: potassium
components.evidence: Dominant mineral

components.name: selenium
components.evidence: Trace

components.name: boron
components.evidence: Genuine plant micronutrient; retained (was in uncovered list), not quantified in USDA

components.name: vitamin A (RAE)
components.evidence: From provitamin-A carotenoids below

components.name: beta-carotene
components.evidence: Main provitamin-A carotenoid of red pepper

components.name: lycopene
components.evidence: Present in red fruit (variable, often low; capsanthin is the dominant red pigment but lacks a BiGG met); not quantified in USDA 170108

components.name: gamma-tocopherol
components.evidence: Minor tocopherol

remove.name: Arsenate
remove.reason: Non-nutritive toxic trace element; not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Rubidium
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace element (K analogue), not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Vanadium
remove.reason: Non-nutritive trace element at food matrix

remove.name: Co2+ / Cobalt
remove.reason: Sub-trace; no vitamin B12 in a plant food so no metabolic role here; FooDB noise

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: Sub-trace contaminant-level; not a defined growth nutrient of this food

missing_added: citric acid (dominant organic acid, was absent)
D-fructose and D-glucose with real amounts (fructose > glucose; sucrose ~0 at full ripeness)
full USDA amino-acid profile with amounts
keep_uncovered: capsanthin (dominant red carotenoid, no BiGG met)
notes: Ripe red sweet (bell) Capsicum annuum. Dominant carbon/energy sources for a microbe: the free hexoses D-fructose (~2.3 g) and D-glucose (~1.9 g) plus citric acid (~0.35 g); sucrose is essentially gone at full ripeness. Organic acids (citric >> malic, fumaric, 2-oxoglutarate, oxalic) were entirely missing from the FooDB profile and are added here; their absolute values carry more uncertainty than the USDA panel because organic-acid quantities derive from Capsicum HPLC ranges (PMC9871545) rather than a single Italian-red measurement. Vitamin C is exceptionally high (~128 mg/100g). Protein/fat are low; carotenoids and phenolics are abundant but mostly non-BiGG (kept as uncovered).
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 170108 (Peppers, sweet, red, raw; SR Legacy)
USDA FoodData Central FDC 2345321 (Pepper, sweet, red, raw; Foundation Foods; individual sugars)
Comprehensive Capsicum annuum fruit-quality / organic-acid assessment, PMC9871545 (citric, malic, fumaric, 2-oxoglutarate, oxalic ranges)
Sugars and ripening in Capsicum, Plants 2023, doi:10.3390/plants12020231 (PMC9863480)
Biochemical characterization of traditional Italian (Campania) sweet peppers, PMC7346132 (ascorbic acid, phenolics in red varieties)
FooDB FOOD00881 (base record, betaine, boron, pigment presence)
